desc:
An ancient road follows the river to the east and west. A short stretch of grass
separates the road from the river, and a recently repaired dry stone wall to the
north stops animals straying from the fields onto the road. A reasonably new
looking wooden gate allows access to the fields without having to climb over the
wall, and even the road here looks in reasonable repair.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
A big cliff begins here. The road follows its top to the east, and to the west,
it continues along the bank of the Estelin River. To the north there is a small
field, which seems to be abandoned and some tents can be seen at the edge of a
small copse.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road runs from the east to the west on the top of a sharp cliff. The sight
towards the south is expansive and you can see the whole Estelin Valley. To the
north, after some fields there is a small copse, and at its edge there are some
tents. You spot a small path leading down to a landing near the water.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road is on the top of a high cliff. There is a small path that goes north,
towards the edge of a small forest, and to the east a very old bridge stands
between the two sides of a deep gorge. The bridge is very old and seems to be a
bit dangerous, especially if you try to travel across it with a mount.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
This bridge is extremely old, and has been partially destroyed both by the
rigours of time, and by the deleterious ways of the armies of Sauron. The first
part of the bridge, near to this side of the gorge, seems still to be solid, but
further across it appears that it may be less able to support much weight.
name: The Crumbled Bridge

desc:
This is the main entrance gate to the once magnificent elven city called
Ost-in-Edhil. Most of the city is in ruins now after a fierce battle and many
years of decay. The Hollin Road itself continues to the west and to the north of
here. It is pretty silent up here on this big cliff that the fallen city rests
on except for some birds here and there, and the rivers that are joining below
you. The Sirannon joins the Glanduin, who just a bit further away gets even more
strengthend by the Sir Lantalaith. They all join their forces and continue their
flow westwards.
name: Ost-in-Edhil's Gate

desc:
The roads bends here. It runs to the south where it enter a big crumbled city.
West and north, there is a deep gorge from where the noise of flowing waters can
be heard. The road seems to continue for forever towards the east, and some
strange building can be seen on the other side of the gorge, to the west.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road runs east-west here at the top of sharp cliff. The vacuum opens just
south of here and one can hear the slow flow of a large river. South-west of
here there are the ruins of a big city, and north of here, a small strange
decaying building blocks any movement in that direction.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road leads westward to a big, old city; eastwards, one can not see its end.
South of here there is a deep precipice in which a slow river flows North of
here there are plenty of old fields, now no more cultivated. A strange building
is north-west of here and far in the north-east one can see a small black hill.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
On the top of a high cliff, the Hollin Road leads east-west. From here, there is
a wonderful sight on all the south bank of the Sirannon, and the remains of a
small dunlending village which seems to have been burnt down recently can be
seen there. West of here, there is a big city, and far to the north one can see
a small black hill.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The cliff is now too high to let someone go to the south, towards the Sirannon
River. The road leads east-west on the top of this cliff and far in the west a
big city appears in the mist. North of the road there is some field, and the
plain that lies in the south is inaccessible from here.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The track follows the riverbed of the Sirannon. Many tracks leading up and down
the track can be seen. The mountains to the east, with their eternal snows, are
rising up. Just south of here the cold Sirannon flows westwards.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
This broad track, which is now known as the Hollin Road, continues to the west
and east, forming a natural border between the northern wetlands and the river
Sirannon to the south. While travel through the wetlands, to the north, is
possible, not only does the soft land make it cumbersome, the faint baying of
nearby wolf-packs makes it less than inviting.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
This rough road, which was onced used by the Elves of Eregion and the Dwarves of
Moria in their travels from Ost-in-Edhil to Moria still runs alongside the river
of Sirannon. While old and decayed, the road is still the quickest path for
traveling across the harsh countryside. From here, the road continues to the
south and east. In the west there are some fields, damp with moisture, and
covered by yellowing vegetation.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The track continues east and west across the sparse wetlands. To the south and
east the mighty river Sirannon borders the track. Even from here, the water
which forms the river Sirannon looks clear and refreshing. Small animals can be
seen taking advantage of the river, some to wash their food, others to quench
their thirst and still more to take a relaxing swim.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The flow of the mighty river Sirannon, which is to the immediate south, can be
heard along this decaying road. Further to the east the track bends slightly to
the north, but to the west it continues in a straight line. To the south, down
the steep bank and across the river, a small stone building of some sort can be
seen and to the north a small pond blocks movement in that direction.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road continues to the west and northeast of here. To the south you can see
an area of flat riverbank where, at one time, the mighty river Sirannon must
have overflowed causing the banks to erode to the extent that the river has
become wide and shallow, making crossing easy, though somewhat tedious. To the
far north an area of burnt ground can be seen. A strange smell comes from that
direction.
name: North of the Stepping Stones

desc:
The Hollin road, a wide well-trodden track, leads across the wetland from the
southeast to the northwest. Farther to the south flows the clear water of the
river Sirannon as it passes through a narrow ravine. On the other bank, some
sort of stone building has been erected. It would have been difficult to cross
the river here, but since numerous stepping stones has been placed across the
ravine, it is no problem.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
In this region the decaying road is quite stony as it continues to the north and
west. The moorland can be seen bordering the road to the north and, again,
further to the south beyond the nearby river. While at one time the river was
quite high, right now there is only an average flow of water.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road bends to the south and east of here as the road winds its way through
the bleak wetland that extends far to the north and west. Even further to the
southeast, across the far bank of the riverbed, you see patches of the misty
land enveloped in some low-lying mist.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
A bend in the track leads to the north and west of here, carrying you across the
bleak moorland and its cold, damp atmosphere. The riverbed to the south runs
parallel to the track, seeming to hug it at every bend and corner, and once sent
a mighty spray onto unwary travelers when it was full which is no longer the
case.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The Hollin moor lies to the north and east of this rough stony road which seems
to continue forever towards the south-west and north-east. To the far east the
peaks of the Misty mountains are visible, and nearer, just across the river to
the south, a small building can be seen.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The sinuous river flows around some boulders here. But on closer examination,
the rocks seem to be cylindrical and possibly made of marble. The Glanduin's
course is unaffected by these boulders as it flows to the west. A healthy green
forest can be seen beyond an open grassy area to the south.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The road following the riverbed just north of here begins to raise slowly,
letting a beautiful wall of carved stone appears at the north bank of the river.
The wall continues far in the east where it seems to joins an old ruined city,
perched on a high rock. Some fields can be seen south of here.
name: The Estelin

desc:
The Estelin flows here gently towards the west. The flow is very calm here and
some aquatic vegetation can be seen in the shallow water along the south bank.
The big wall of carved stones to the north lowers slowly and seems to vanish
some distance to the west.
name: The Estelin

desc:
The river flows here between a high carved wall to the north and a low riverbank
to the south. The Estelin is a good-sized river at this point - the mountains
wherein its source lies can barely be made out against the horizon.
name: The Estelin

desc:
The two rivers mix seamlessly, one current smoothly merges with the other. The
crystalline water of the Sir Lantalaith lightens the water of the green Estelin
only slightly. South of here fields can be made out. The gorge to the north is
spanned by two bridges - one made of planks, and the other, higher bridge made
of carved stones.
name: Confluence of the Sir Lantalaith and Estelin

desc:
The lazy waves of the Estelin run along big carved rocks, on whose summit the
ruins of an ancient city can be spied. The city is probably very badly decayed,
as portions of it seem to have collapsed into the river over the years.
name: The Estelin

desc:
The waters of these two rivers mixed here. Lots of eddies makes any boating or
swimming dangerous : it is like the two rivers are fighting together. The
Estelin continues its flow toward the west and north of here is the Sirannon
River.
name: Confluence of the Sirannon and Glanduin

desc:
The waters wash against the base of a small cliff to the west. Atop the slope,
the crumbling walls of a ruined city can be seen. North of here, there is
another cliff, and there appears to be a road running along its summit. To the
south, the Sirannon joins forces with another much larger river.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
A wide open field slopes slightly downhill towards the river to the south.
Although there are the remains of many dry stone walls all over the field, to
the south, just before the road, is one of the few still intact walls. With a
wooden gate hanging in a gap in the wall to the south, it looks like someone has
repaired it fairly recently.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The river is not far from an old city, which seems to block the riverbed at some
miles in the east. A big wall comes out of the city, supporting what should be a
very important road, as the wall - partially destroyed - is several yards high.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The waters are doing some eddies here, but they do not seem to be dangerous.
Except for that, the Sirannon is a very calm river flowing toward the east.
North of here a very big wall, partially destroyed, seems to support a road.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
An artificial wall, constructed long ago by the Noldor or Dwarves, prevents one
from leaving the river to the north. The south bank is covered with burnt hulls
of what were once huts. The flow of the river runs slowly toward the west, where
some imposing ruins can be seen.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The lazy flow of the Sirannon runs to the west here. An old road, following the
bed of the river to the north, begins to rise letting an artificial wall
building an unclimbable slope at some distance of the north. Small burned down
huts are visible on the south bank of the river.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The river Sirannon continues its east-west flow, bringing life to the lands of
Hollin. The river has a brisk pace, but is not so fast that it cannot be
navigated in safety. To the north of the river lies a well-trodden road which
runs parallel to the river for as far as you can see, and beyond the road the
northern wetlands of Hollin. To the south, the wetlands continue interspersed by
a few nearby hills.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The river continues its east-west flow, the current slow and calm, as the river
widens. A bit of spray can be seen where the clear water cascades over some
rocks. To the north you can see an old and decayed road that was once well used
by people traveling through the Hollin. The foliage of the area is typical of
this region; fields of vegetation dotted by numerous holly bushes and small
trees. To the south, the rise of small hills can be seen.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The river continues to the east and west of here, shaping the land as it flows.
The Hollin road, to the north, can be seen matching the river's every bend and
turn. To the south however, you can see the wetlands which cover the area south
of the river. To the southeast you can see the corner of a stone building which
is adjacent to the bank of the river. The building does not look sturdy, and
appears as if it may fall into the river at any moment.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The old river flows from the west, swiftly past the riverbank, towards the east,
past a shallow area before turning to the north. While it is possible to climb
up the steep northern bank, it will take much effort to do so. To the southeast
and southwest a few small huts can be seen. The hills to the far south obstruct
your vision in that direction while to the far north you can see the wetlands
which are typical of this region.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The waters flows slowly from the north towards the south where the river goes
under two bridges. The higher one seems partially destroyed, but the lower one,
made of some wooden planks, seems more recently built and, therefore safer. The
sides of the gorge are carved, and were also painted, also the colors have
mostly disappeared.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
This tiny path comes out from the Hollin Road which runs east-west just south of
here. It leads north along a small river to the east. The river lies far below
the path and a small barrier prevents anybody from falling down. Plains lie
beyond the river, as well as far to the north, where the path seems to be
blocked by some kind of a building.
name: A Narrow Track

desc:
The sight from this vantage point is an unusual one - the river flows between
the carved walls of the gorge to the south, where it is crossed by two bridges.
The higher bridge is made of stone, while the other bridge is situated only a
few yards above the waters.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
The river flows calmly here. It seems to have some rapids in the north where
some blocks have fallen in the river, but here the river is very quiet: one can
even see the ground through the clear water. The river bends here toward the
east; the sides of the gorge, always carved, seem unclimbable.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
The path makes a small bend here. It leads westward to some small huts that are
visible in the distance, and to the south it rejoins a larger road. Eastward
there is a river, but the water lies several yards below the lip of the bank. To
the north there appears to be an ancient cemetery.
name: A Bend in a Narrow Track

desc:
As soon the outskirts of this village are passed, you notice the smell of
something recently burned down. It looks like this village has been plundered
and destroyed not long ago. There are no signs of any villagers left alive. The
village was made up of very small sod huts which now are more or less wrecked.
The village path continues to the west from here and there are plains east of
here.
name: A Village Entrance

desc:
On arrival at the centre of the village you notice no signs of any living
creatures whatsoever. Everybody seems to have either died or abandoned the
remains of this village. The house to the west seems to have been somewhat of a
regal air to it and is certainly grander looking than the rest of the houses. To
the south is a the remains of a small sod hut.
name: Centre of a Destroyed Village

desc:
The inside of this once big hut is quite small but considerably more spacious
than the others of this village. The ceiling has been completely perforated and
someone has dug holes in the floor obviously looking for something. Most of the
walls have been razed. The remains of a large fireplace lie in the centre of
this annihilated hut.
name: A Dilapidated Hut

desc:
Green grass covers the land around here and the pollen in the air makes you feel
quite drowsy. Farm animals graze quietly around without it would seem paying
much attention to you. Every now and again something will raise its head and
give you a discerning look before going back to whatever it was doing
originally. To the south the leftovers of a burnt down small sod hut sit
quietly. Grassy plains open up to the east and to the west is the once lively
village.
name: Village Pasture

desc:
This muddy region of the riverbank has eroded, leaving only a few tufts of
bright green vegetation. You can enter the river by going north down a shallow
slope and just beyond the river you can make out the broad Hollin road. To the
south the northern reaches of Dunland can be found. However - to the west you
can see the landscape shifts into more farmland.
name: Riverbank of the Sirannon

desc:
Here, the river has become rather shallow making it easy to cross. To the south
you can barely make out a track that seems to lead towards a stone building. The
side of the river bank indicates that you are not the first person to have
forded the river here in order to enter the northern reaches of the Dunland. The
river flows east and west from here.
name: Shallow Sirannon

desc:
This area of the moor is overgrown with young holly bushes, but beneath the
young branches of the bushes the track is well defined. It is hard to tell
whether the track was made by man or animals. To the north lies the Sirannon,
forever flowing to the west. From here you can make out the faint outlines of a
structure to the south, though the swaying branches of the young holly bushes
obscure your vision. If you were to remove the branches from the bushes, you
would have a clear view of a poorly constructed stone building. The doorway of
the building would look worn and in need of repair, and you would be able to see
recent tracks upon the path that leads to the building's entrance that would
imply that the building was still in use. While the building would not appear to
be in good condition, it would appear to provide better shelter than the
branches of the bushes that obscure your vision.
name: Overgrown Track

desc:
Standing at the edge of a large stone building, you can see a doorway set in the
wall further to the east. The riverbed of the Sirannon, which is to the north,
flows from west to east carrying its life giving water as it shadows the
decaying road which lies beyond the northern bank. To the west a small decaying
hut can be seen and to the south a few hills dot the landscape.
name: Riverbank of the Sirannon

desc:
To the north the River Sirannon can be heard flowing, while to the west a stone
dwelling has been built upon the riverbank, making it somewhat difficult for you
to approach the front of the building. You can, however, follow a small track
which leads to the building entrance. The rough stone walls of the building are
covered with dew, and to the west, beyond the building wetlands are visible.
name: Riverbank of the Sirannon

desc:
This corner of the grassy wetland, bordered by the river Sirannon to the north
and west and a small building to the east, seems to be well tended for the
vegetation is green and healthy, ready for harvest. A line of holly bushes are
growing at the edge of the field forming a natural fence between the field and
the nearby dwelling. A strong odor comes from the building, indicating a lack of
hygiene by the current inhabitant.
name: Well-tended Wetland

desc:
The holly bushes growing upon this section of the moor seem to be very hearty.
The branches are well formed and are heavy with inkberries. An odor of decaying
feces comes the nearby dwelling which is likely to be the home of whomever tends
to these somewhat groomed plants.
name: Beside the Decaying Hut

desc:
Tall grasses obscure the view in any direction here. The occasional thistle
makes wading through the grasses very uncomfortable. An occasional animal trail
can be seen winding its way across the plain.
name: Tall Grasses

desc:
Grasses grow quite tall on this part of the grassy plain. From here there is a
great view of the land to the southwest. The plain has been broken up into many
fields by some stone walls, and animals can be seen grazing in them. Further
away the Glanduin traces its way from east to west. A path seems to follow the
river.
name: Grassy Field with a View

desc:
A large pond lies in the middle of this field, and many animal tracks lead up to
it. It looks as if sheep and cows are the most frequent users, but there could
be other animals too. Dry stone walls denote the edges of fields, although in
places the wall has collapsed, allowing the animals to roam free.
name: Pond in the Grassy Field

desc:
High stone walls have been built around this part of the plain. However, many
sections have fallen down allowing access from most of the nearby fields. Not
far to the south a road follows the river Glanduin from east to west.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
The chief of the thieves should hide the treasures in that room. But, at the
moment, it is quite empty. The walls are only plain earth and a ladder leads up.
The air is a little moisty. Some empty chests can be seen along the northern
wall.
name: The Chief's Cache

desc:
The thief who lives here will surely not be a small robber. This tent is filled
with lots of costly furniture. A fine carpet lies on the ground, covering most
of it.
name: The Chief's Tent

desc:
Tall thorns seem to throw their sharp spikes and spears at you as you progress
up this path. From here the path continues north and south, and from a dark wood
in the east some spine-chilling shrieks and howls can be heard from time to
time.
name: A Dark Path

desc:
Huge, impenetrable walls of thorns blockade all further passage in any direction
except from where you came. To the east, a tall wall of thorny bushes blocks the
way, like in the north and the west. The only exit is back to the south onto the
trail.
name: A Dead End

desc:
This encampment consists of a few tents. A big fireplace stands in the middle of
them. Some trunks were thrown all around it, letting people sit comfortably
around the fire. The huts themselves are without interest, except one, just
north of here which is much bigger than the other.
name: Thieves' Camp

desc:
A tall wall of thorns blocks any further passage to the north and the west. Even
though trying and keeping the thorns as far away as possible from you, it is not
possible to pass through it, even by scratching your body on the sharp points.
The only possible exits are east or south.
name: A Thorny Wood

desc:
The trail here peters out, with a few tracks continuing to the dark path that
leads north of here. To the east a huge wall of thornbushes blocks your way over
which you can barely make out a massive dark looking building. The trail
continues north and south here.
name: An Old Disused Trail

desc:
This cemetery has been abandoned for a long time. All of the tombs - and there
are plenty of them - are covered with moist moss. Some of them have collapsed
into piles of stone with weeds growing among the rubble. It seems that someone
has been here not long ago, for there are recent tracks and the remnants of
quite a few fires. A few of the tombs look to have been pried open - apparently
by thieves in search of treasure.
name: The Cemetery

desc:
Small shafts of light that penetrate through the woody canopy above you give you
just enough light to navigate by. From time to time small woodland creatures run
past you on their daily business of hunting or being hunted. From here the wood
continues east and north and a large hedgerow blocks any sight, and any walk, to
the south.
name: Dense Woodland

desc:
A small, well-worn path leads east and west here, and some old tracks lead to
the north as well. To the west in the distance some small buildings can be made
out. Northeast is a small copse hiding the rest of the valley. All around, the
grass has been cropped short by grazing animals.
name: A Narrow Track

desc:
From here the plains seem to stretch on to eternity, like a sea of green the
grass slowly waves back and forth with calm undulations under a light breeze
that blows across the plains.
name: A Grassy Plain

desc:
All around are small herds of animals quietly grazing away and seemingly
oblivious to your presence. The grass has been cropped short here by the animals
grazing, but the ground is still very soft underfoot, with patches of mud about
where too many animals have stood for too long. To the south only the waters of
the Sirannon can be seen coming out of an artificial gorge.
name: A Grassy Plain

desc:
This part of the cemetery seems older than the rest. There are no traces of
thieves or graverobbers here - all the tombs appear intact. Most of them are
engulfed in high weeds and brambles. A tall hedge blocks movement in every
direction save west.
name: The Old Cemetery

desc:
Several large rocks have fallen down from the two carved walls that form the
gorge in which the Ringnen flows. Lots of foam floats on the water, which rushes
at a furious pace. The river flows onward to the south.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
The Sir Lantalaith flows here between two carved walls, making a deep gorge.
These walls are very old and partially destroyed. The waters flows quite
quickly, but it should not be too dangerous to boat or to swim around here. Due
to some foam, it is not possible to see the bed of the river.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
In that small copse, you can hear birds singing above you in the tree canopy.
Around you lie a few rotting tree trunks amongst other foliage. From here the
wood seems to continues to the west getting slightly darker as further you look.
The plains are east of here.
name: A Small Wood

desc:
In the distance to the east there is a small copse, although from here it looks
quite dark and gloomy. The local wildlife seems to enjoy an idyllic existence,
and there is lots of evidence of small mammals who must make their homes in the
grass. The plains continue to the north and west. A large unscalable wall blocks
access to the river, which flows beneath in a deep gorge.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The river flows towards the southwest and enters a deep gorge just south of
here. The waters steadily gain speed, and some froth begins to be visible. The
sharp cliffs loom ominously over the river to the south.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
Along some fields in the east and the west, the waters of the Sir Lantalaith
begins here to become wilder. The two banks are narrower than in the north, and
the speed of the river is thus increased. The river continues south-west from
here between two stone walls, and one can see a small copse far in the west and
a strange black hill not far in the south-east.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
The quiet waters of the Sir Lantalaith bend here toward the south where they
disappear in a deep gorge. Some fields can be seen on the west bank and far
there is a small wood. The east bank is also covered by some fields and very far
in the east one can see the barrier of the Misty Mountains.
name: Sir Lantalaith

desc:
The water has a clear blue hue of purity as it flows from the rocks. Fish swim
about, darting among the shallow pools. From this point, the river flows to the
west, winding away to join the Glanduin. To the south, some old fields can be
seen.
name: Eithel Lantalaith

desc:
In the near distance there is a small copse. Every now and again a slight
movement catches your eye making you glance over in that direction. For some
reason unknow to you the grass is slightly shorter here almost as if it had been
grazed by some herd animal. The wood is at some distance west of here and a
small river stands in the east.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
In the distance to the west there is a small, dark wood. From here it looks very
unfriendly and foreboding, although here in the plains all is calm and peaceful.
Small animals dart past your feet and disappear into the undergrowth. The plains
stretch out to the south and the east.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The wood is quite dense here, but the ground can still be seen. The ground is
quite moist and you leave footprints where your feet have been. All around can
be seen the tracks of other woodland creatures as they criss-crossed your path.
The wood continues west and south, there is a plain to the east.
name: A Small Wood

desc:
This small room has some carved walls with ugly paintings and sculptures on
them. There is also the rest of eight columns in the centre of it, which seem to
be much older than the rest of the temple. They should have each a figure on it,
but all are destroyed and only some writing can still be seen on them.
name: The Rests of Argonbar

desc:
Some wheat can sparsely be seen in that field, indicating that this was a
previouly cultivated area. North of here stretch some vast plains. A strange
temple stands just west of here and the Hollin Road is south of here, running
east-west. A small hill, completely black, can be seen in the north-east.
name: An Old Field

desc:
This tomb is quite big, it seems that its builders constructed it carefully, in
order to let it last for ages. The walls are carved, but no painting can be seen
on it. The tomb continues to the the north and to the west where the tomb seems
to be in a worse state of preservation.
name: Entrance of a Tomb

desc:
This room was originally the place where the corpse of a dwarf was deposited in
order to wait for the end of the time, until the soul will come back from the
Halls of Mandos. The corpse was originally lying in a wooden sarcophage, but was
thrown away from it by some grave looter who then pillaged it. The walls,
slightly carved, remain untouched. The only obvious exit leads to the south.
name: A Tomb

desc:
This part of the tomb is crumbling, and even small animals like rats fear it. As
soon as you enter, the noise you make create some vibrations that make some
parts of the already broken ceiling to fall down, hitting you some. Nothing can
be seen in this section, either it is gone since very long ago, or it has been
destroyed by falling rocks during all these years.
name: A Decaying Tomb

desc:
The marble tunnel ends here. It is not a wall that blocks your way, but a huge
pile of bones from molested skeletons. Skulls and pieces of bones lie scattered
in the shallow water. The scent of dead tissue is intense but lots of worms and
maggots seem to like the atmosphere since they cover the marble walls.
name: Tunnel under the Cemetery

desc:
The water covering the ground is dark and cold. The marble walls are wet from
the humidity of the air in the tunnel. A dead bat is floating in the water. Some
reflections in the water indicate that there is an open room just up of here.
The tunnel also continues southwards.
name: Tunnel under the Cemetery

desc:
Moisture covers the marble walls in this corridor. The muddy ground is sloping
downwards to your south. To the north the corridor expands in a larger room. The
humid air carries a scent of death and decay. The ribcage of a human is lying on
the ground along with some fragments of an arm.
name: A Corridor

desc:
The smell is very strange here, the air must be a little bit different than
above ground. It means that this tomb is probably very old and rarely, perhaps
never, visited by living creatures. No corpse can be seen here and the floor
goes slowly down towards the south.
name: In an Old Tomb

desc:
The grass is not very high here and some tufts of yellow, some wheat, can be
seen. Just west of here is a deep gorge where a small river flows and east of
here a bare mound of earth stands in a field, hiding most part of the mountains
that are very far in the east. North of here there is a small river and the
field continues in the south.
name: An Old Field

desc:
In the middle of this field lies a slightly mounded patch of barren earth. The
area around the mound appears to be infertile - the few clumps of vegetation
that have managed to grow are yellowed and have a sickly appear- ance. The
fields themselves have lain fallow for centuries - no one has cultivated here
for more than a millennium.
name: Haudh-en-Morgwaith

desc:
This field stands in a bend of the river which disappears into a deep gorge just
south-west of here. Some sparse wheat grows here and there, but the field is no
longer cultivated. The Misty Mountains can be seen very far in the east, and the
middle of the field south seems to be barren.
name: An Old Field

desc:
This field stands just north of the Hollin Road. The weeds are high, and some
yellow spots can be seen, it is some wheat that still grows here after years of
fallow. A strange black hill stands just north of here.
name: An Old Field

desc:
Stairs carved in the soil lead to the muddy bottom of a rather large burrow. It
extends in all directions allowing you to move around freely. The chilly water
covering the mud emits a penetrating scent of decay. An almost completely intact
skeleton of a fallen minion of Sauron lies before you.
name: Below Haudh-en-Morgwaith

desc:
This section is rather narrow and is not as well-excavated at the rest. You have
to force your way through the roots and the mounds of earth on the floor. There
are no parts of skeletons here. This area of the grave was never used.
name: Western Burrow of Haudh-en-Morgwaith

desc:
The burrow is very irregular. The walls are covered with marks from the fingers
of its builders. The chilly green water still cover the soft and muddy ground.
Clods of earth hang in the roots that grow through the ceiling.
name: Northern Burrow of Haudh-en-Morgwaith

desc:
The roots hanging in the ceiling whip you face as you enter a narrower section
of the burrow. Bones that once were arms or legs stick up from the mud. The
stench is so intense is almost completely absorbs your attention. The burrow
continues westwards.
name: Eastern Burrow of Haudh-en-Morgwaith

desc:
This is the southernmost part of this underground burrow. A huge root grows
straight from the ceiling to the muddy floor. It seems extremely solid and was
probably not worth the effort to chop off. There are marks from the hands of the
builders in the wall. Workers with large hands built this part of the burrow.
name: Southern Burrow of Haudh-en-Morgwaith

desc:
Some sparse wheat grows here, indicating that this field was cultivated long
ago. In every direction, there is such field, except south, where a decaying
road can be seen. Very far in the west, a city seems to come out of the ground.
name: An Old Field

desc:
This field was cultivated long time ago by the Noldor. During the Second Age, a
big war happened and the followers of Sauron destroyed the area. Now only sparse
wheat can be seen, and the Hollin road, which leads east-west just south of
here, is partially destroyed.
name: An Old Field

desc:
While the wetlands continue to the north, they are separated from the river
Sirannon to the south, by the wide Hollin road. Looking carefully at the ground,
the remains of the bones of those who were either too weak or too slow to leave
this area can be seen. The northern edge of the Dunland lies just beyond of the
river Sirannon and offers shelter to those that come in friendship.
name: Wetland beside the Track

desc:
To the north and south lay the Hollin wetlands making movement in those
directions difficult. However, in the distant south a track across the wetlands
is faintly visible. To the west a thick tangle of holly bushes makes movement in
that direction difficult if not impossible. Upon the soft ground the prints of
those who have passed before you are embedded.
name: Fields of Hollin

desc:
A tangle of prickly holly bushes makes passage to the north and west impossible,
but to the south and east, passage is possible. Further to the southeast a
smooth track, which forms the Hollin road, can be seen, beckoning for travel.
You can hear the baying of wolves in the distance, but are unable to tell which
direction the sound originates from.
name: Beside a Prickly Hedge

desc:
Tall, green grass cushions your feat as you stroll idly across these plains. The
plains gently roll and undulate, stretching off into the horizon to the north
and the east. The wind softly sways the grass in gentle eddies, cooling your
face and giving you crisp, fresh air to breath. There is a small river just west
of here.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The grass is green and very lush here, gently brushing past your knees as you
wade through it. The grass is very soft and has the calming effect of soothing
your feet. Around you there are animals grazing and insects buzzing around
flowers in search of nectar. The plains disappear off the horizon. Far in the
west there is a small river.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The path towards the south is blocked by a thick hedge of holly bushes. However,
you can easily move across the lightly wooded area to the west and the plains to
the east. To the north, you can see a large flat plain sparsely populated with
trees and overrun by oxen and horses.
name: A Grove in Hollin Fields

desc:
The luxuriant fields of Hollin extend north and west from where you stand. The
former Elven cultivators are gone. Wild herds of oxen, horses and deers now roam
the fields which were once used to feed Ost-in-Edhil some 4500 years ago. A
large pond, formed from a shallow depression and filled by the recent rains, is
east of here. The mark of hooves show that animals use the pond as a source of
water. Southwards a line of birch trees mark the bank of the river Sirannon.
name: Hollin Fields

desc:
To the south of here a thin plume of smoke can be seen, curling slowly upward in
the wind. The wetland becomes very damp to the west while a smooth track lies
directly to the south. You can see the remains of some small animals decaying on
the rough grass.
name: Hollin Wetland

desc:
Dank and rotten undergrowth abounds here, the decomposing organics emits a
strong odor and makes the ground soft and mushy. Your feet are sucked into the
deep mud, which continues more towards the north than towards the west, making
it difficult if not impossible to move in those directions. Drier land, however,
can be found to the east and south making it much easier to move in those
directions. A smooth track lies towards the southeast of here.
name: Fields of Hollin

desc:
There are many small holly bushes here, some dotted with a few sickly red
berries, but the majority of them barren. Other than the bushes, there are a few
strands of yellow vegetation, much of it dead, the rest dying. In the distant
east and south, beyond the wetlands, the track of the Hollin Road is faintly
visible. Unseen from here, to the far south, lies the Dunland and to the north
and west drier land can be found.
name: Bright Bushes in the Wetlands

desc:
The plain slowly slopes towards the south and extends as far as one can see in
all directions. The only clear landmark that can be seen from here is a line of
dense vegetation to the distant south, where the river Sirannon is known to
flow. The vegetation growing here is a mixture of wild grains such as oat and
wheat. Wild grapes can be seen growing at the foot of a low fallen stonewall,
indicating that this plain was once a part of the fertile fields of Hollin which
was first cultivated by the elves of Eregion and later by the men of Arnor.
name: Hollin Fields

desc:
You can tell that this corner of grassy wetland was carefully tended to by
someone, for some small holly bushes grow in a neat row along the edge of a low
stone wall forming a fence which blocks movement towards the south. The stone
wall are the only remaining debris of an old farm. To the east the river
Sirannon can be heard, but a flooded plain blocks the direct path.
name: Fields of Hollin

desc:
Things are very quiet here, almost unnaturally quiet, causing a sense of unease
which warns you that danger may be nearby. A campsite, which appears to be
abandoned, lies towards the east. To the distant south lies a track which would
ease your travel along this hilly ground. While the wetlands continue to the
west, a dense row of small bushes prevents movement in that direction.
name: West of an Abandoned Camp

desc:
This camp has obviously been raided by a group of marauding nomads recently.
Fresh blood covers the ground around a burnt-out fire, and discarded arrows
still litter the place. When you get near the burnt-out fire, you can still feel
some heat coming from it. Maybe the nomads are still near? From the feathers on
the arrows one would suspect that the attackers might have been one of the
infamous tribes from the northern Dunland.
name: Abandoned Camp

desc:
An unusual smell from the west pervades the air here. The moorland seems the
same as those surrounding you but a faint smell of blood suggests that some deed
of violence has been committed nearby. The moor continues to the east and a
decaying road crosses the hilly lands to the south.
name: East of an Old Camp

desc:
Here, the moorland has sparse vegetation, enough to keep the top soil from
washing away but little more. The lack of vegetation is due to the rocky soil,
which is unusual for this region of the Eregion which is known for its great
fertility. To the south, a rough track which forms the Hollin Road, can be seen.
Further to the west a thin column of smoke can be seen as it slowly drifts in
the wind.
name: Hollin Moor

desc:
The rolling hills abound here are covered with vegetation of all shapes and
sizes, most of it in the form of low-lying holly bushes and heather. Across the
moorland to the south you can just make out a wide track which winds across the
land. Just south of the track, a depression in the earth is seen. Probably the
small ravine which forms the river Sirannon.
name: Rolling Moorland

desc:
The road continues straight, running east and west of here, as the clear waters
of the River Sirannon flow swiftly past. On the opposite bank there seems to be
some sort of cairn which can be seen quite clearly against the dark moorland
background. To the south a small area of the bank seems to slope gently down to
the edge of the water.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
Years of usage has caused deep ruts to form, making the track very rough. Even
though, travel upon the track is far faster than moving across the damp
moorlands which surrounds the track. The track bends to the north and west here,
as it passes north of a flat section of land which is the bank to the river
Sirannon, and leads toward the mountains to the far east.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
A broad road crosses the land and continues south and east of here. To the south
of here the river Sirannon can be heard and beyond the river a large cairn can
be seen, standing proud in the bleak surroundings of the moors.
name: Bend in the Hollin Road

desc:
Here, the river continues straight east and west as it winds through the
wetlands. The river runs parallel to a wide track which crosses the land north
of here, and leads on into the distance. When looking further to the south you
notice some sort of a stone cairn that has been built near the bank of the
river. A few hills can be spotted in the far reaches of the area, both south and
north, but they are a ways off.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
Here, the river flows swiftly, being fed continually by mountain snowmelt and
rainfall. The bottom of the river is covered with smooth stones, worn down by
years of moving water. The channel that bears the current is not overly deep,
making it easy to clamber up to the north where a small track runs. To the
south, some hills can be seen, covered in the typical foliage of this region.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
Directly to the north of here, you notice a shallow sloping bank which provides
easy access to the road. The river flows swiftly on to the south-west and
north-east, though not with the force of larger rivers such as the Bruinin. A
stone structure and some small huts are visible to the south of the river.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The torrent water flows swiftly over the boulders, which are pounded by the full
might of the River Sirannon. The great abundance of fishes and other water
creatures makes for a lively scene, darting around and amongst various aquatic
plants. You can continue along the channel to the north and west, but the
erosion has chopped the banks at a steep angle, making travel to the east and
south difficult.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
Here, the flow of the Sirannon bends slightly to the north and west, as it runs
parallel to the Hollin road which is just north of here. The shallow slope on
the northern bank seems an ideal way to leave the riverbed. A bit to the south,
you spy a series of small dwellings that are obviously very old. To get a better
view however, you will have to go beyond the ravine formed by the river.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
You notice that the moorland in the distant northwest are quite overgrown, but
to the southwest they are more sparse as they become enshrouded by a dense mist.
A small stone hut of some kind lies to the southwest and adjacent to the stone
building is a small hut. The bed of the river, itself, continues to the north
and east. Though the land is fairly flat here, it seems to be but a small
plateau as the region to the southeast becomes hilly. The water flows swiftly
but peacefully along the river.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The river Sirannon continues west and bends north at this point, in its ever
winding path. A rough road can be seen to the north, running adjacent to the
river, leading through the wetlands. Small hills are visible to the east, both
north and south of here. The southern Misty Mountains, with it's three peaks -
Caradhras, Celebdil and Fanuidhol - loom in the very distant east.
name: Bend in the Sirannon

desc:
Just to the north of here, a large pile of stones forms a cairn on the south
bank of the Sirannon. The moorland stretches to the south, but to the west a
rough shelter has been constructed. Getting inside the shelter from here will
require a bit of travel for it is still a few miles away. The moorlands have
been cleared of vegetation, and rough trenches have been dug into the soil.
Perhaps someone is attempting to cultivate some crops.
name: Hills South of the Cairn

desc:
A small hut made of animal hides seems to stand guard against the wilderness on
this sandy hill. The smell of roasting meat comes from within the hut to the
south and some movement can be heard from there. The ground all around here has
been trodden down by the passage of many people and the mud sticks to your feet
as you pass over it. North-east of here there is an odd pile of stones.
name: Sandy Hill before a Hut

desc:
A building to the south-west of here was constructed of rough stone, with little
skill or attention to details. The gaps between the rough-hewn rocks leave the
rooms inside the building almost as cold as the moorland out here. There is no
entrance to the building on this side however, there must be an entrance to the
building to the west or south of here. To the east, a tent, constructed with
animal skins, stands upon a sandy hill.
name: Near a Sandy Hill

desc:
This area of moorland lies between a strange Cairn to the east, a small building
to the west, and the riverbed of the Sirannon to the north. These moorland seem
wet, having soaked up the excess water that overflow the banks of the nearby
river. Some faint animal tracks are barely visible in the wet mud, leading
around a tent-like hut to the southeast.
name: Moorland on the Bank of the Riverbed

desc:
To the north the moors continues until it reaches the river Sirannon, but to the
south there is a small hut which seems like a suitable resting place before you
contine your journey. The wind carries the faint sound of flowing water in the
north and you can just make out an irregularity in the northern reaches of
Dunland to the east. The nature of the irregularity seems to be a pile of rocks,
next to a small village.
name: Rocky Hollin Moor

desc:
A large stone cairn stands in front of you, crudely constructed with stones from
the surrounding moorlands. From the rough stones you can deduce that the cairn
is very old, but it is not apparent why it was built outside the boundary of the
nearby village. To the north the river Sirannon flows swiftly by, causing a
pleasant spray to be thrown across the banks. Around the cairn, you notice that
vegetation is strangely lacking, almost as if the plants are scared to grow
there.
name: A Large Cairn

desc:
You find the ground here unsound as as you sink to your knees in the damp mud.
Passage through here is very slow as your attempts to get out of this damp area
of moorland are nearly futile. The thought of being attacked here while ensnared
by the mud makes getting out seem even more imperative.
name: Bog in the Moor

desc:
Winding past you to the north is the swift flowing Sirannon. A stone building,
which is to the distant south-west of here, can be seen. An overgrown track
leads away from that doorway towards the north. Another, closer, hut lies to the
south.
name: Moorland by the River

desc:
Here you can see the clear water flowing swiftly past you as the moorland meets
the bank of the river. The water carries leaves and small twigs from nearby
trees down stream as it flows towards the sea. The moorland itself continues to
the south, seemingly featureless except for a strange nearby pile of stones.
name: Moor near the Sirannon

desc:
A small unlit chamber has been carefully carved into the rocks underneath the
somber crypt above. The chamber is very cramped and much of the space is
occupied by the decayed remains of humans who are now eternally at rest free
from the struggles of daily life. The rusted and worthless chain mail garments
which once adorn their bodies mark them as warriors, the crumbling weapons of
their lively hood still clasped in skeletal hands. A scripture has been carved
upon the shield of one of the warriors.
name: Warrior's Final Resting Place

desc:
A bright radiance seems to issue forth from various coffins within this crypt,
reflecting off the dust stirred by your movements. The air gives the whole room
an ethereal quality, seeming to lull you gently, but your sharp senses detect an
evil intent which continues to lurk in the dimmest corners. A dusty stone plaque
is carved into the eastern wall.
name: Hidden Crypt beneath the Moor

desc:
This large natural cavern seems to have been enlarged by skilled masons in order
to fit a slab across the above entrance. Climbing down was easy, once you could
see the rough ladder carved into the wall, but now a feeling of unease washes
over you as you examine the surroundings. Was that a wolf howling outside, or
the cry of a tortured soul from within the cavern ?
name: Cavern under the Cairn

desc:
The passage continues north and south; there is also a tunnel to the east. It is
very dark here, but one can see some light coming from the south. To the east
there is the start of a tunnel, but it soon disappears into the darkness. The
walls of the passage are smooth and have been well mined.
name: A Mine Passage

desc:
This is the base of a ladder. The area surrounding the ladder is lit by torches
hung on the walls but looking into the passage to the south, you are soon
overwhelmed by the darkness. The ladder has been well crafted out of some
hardwood and looks well cared for. The only exits are back up the ladder or the
mine passage to the south.
name: Base of a Ladder

desc:
This seems to be the end of the passage. In the southern wall is a large and
sturdy looking door made out of some dark metal. Around the door, some torches
hang illuminating the whole passage. The passage continues to the north.
name: End of the Passage

desc:
This room is filled with maps and other papers. The dwarves who are working in
this mine have carefully planned their operation in order to maximize the
discovery and extraction of hidden jewels and precious metals from the foothills
of the Misty Mountains. Some of the maps have fallen down and are covering most
of an old tattered carpet.
name: The Mine Operations Room

desc:
You seemed to have reached a nexus in the tunnels. Most of the traffic travels
north and west. The walls are covered in, what presumably are names, in a
language that you can't read. The ways south and west are partially blocked off
with barriers.
name: A Mine Tunnel

desc:
There is something not quite right about the darkness here, it seems to have a
gradient quality to it, sometimes darker in places than others. The walls of the
passage are well finished and the craftsmanship involved hasn't been seen for
many years. There is also another tunnel leading off in that direction,
otherwise the tunnel continues south.
name: A Mine Tunnel

desc:
You enter a small side tunnel. It's very dark in here and the air is full of
dust, making it very difficult to breathe. A few dwarves scurry back and forth
past you, carrying armfulls of rock and ore. The tunnel continues to the west,
and the main tunnel is to the east.
name: A Side Tunnel

desc:
You arrive at what must be the rock face. There are few miners busy digging and
smashing the rock up; they seem to take no notice of you. The air here is very
stale and full of dust making it hard to breathe; you wonder how anyone could
work under these conditions. The tunnel runs back to the east.
name: Rock Face

desc:
This part of the mine is very old : the walls are all very smooth and polished.
The passageway disappears off intho the darkness to the south. The stairs, that
lead up, are made of strong wood. Some torches are firmly fixed onto the wall.
name: At the Base of the Stairs

desc:
Sticky webs span the reach of this large natural cavern. Visibility is limited
but surprisingly beautiful whenever the available light reflects off of the
sticky threads that have been spun. The dessicated corpses of small creatures
can be seen hanging around the room or discarded on the floor.
name: Web-covered Cavern
